questions.q: Sāriputta opens this teaching by naming two categories. What are they, and why begin there?
questions.a: The wholesome (kusala) and the unwholesome (akusala), and the roots of each. He begins there because right view, at the most practical level, is the ability to distinguish the skillful from the unskillful in what one does — and to know the roots (greed/hatred/delusion versus non-greed/non-hatred/non-delusion) that each action is emerging from.

questions.q: Sāriputta does not stop at ethics. He keeps reframing what right view is, layer by layer. Name at least three of the sixteen frameworks he walks through.
questions.a: Wholesome and unwholesome roots; nutriment (āhāra); the Four Noble Truths; aging and death (twelve links of dependent origination); birth; becoming; clinging; craving; feeling; contact; six sense bases; name-and-form; consciousness; formations; ignorance; and the taints (āsava). Each layer is a legitimate way to describe right view — the sutta is showing that right view is not a doctrine but a way of seeing that can be entered from many doors.

questions.q: The sutta repeats a formula for each framework — "when a noble disciple understands X, the origin of X, the cessation of X, and the way leading to the cessation of X..." What is this formula exactly, and where else in the canon does it appear?
questions.a: It is the Four Noble Truths pattern applied to everything. The practitioner does not just know the thing; they know the thing, how it arises, that it can end, and the path to its ending. This is the architecture of every liberating insight in the suttas — phenomenon → origin → cessation → path.

questions.q: The Buddha also taught right view in MN 117 (The Great Forty). What does MN 9 emphasize that MN 117 does not?
questions.a: MN 9 is Sāriputta's exposition — experiential, layered through the full chain of dependent origination, and focused on how right view manifests in concrete ethical discrimination. MN 117 is the Buddha's own teaching — structural, two-tier (mundane and supramundane right view), and focused on how right view relates to the other seven factors of the path. Both are right view; they illuminate different aspects.

questions.q: What is the practical test for whether your right view is real or just intellectual?
questions.a: The sutta frames it through abandonment — when right view is real, the underlying tendencies (anusaya) toward sensual lust, ill will, the view of self, doubt, and ignorance are progressively abandoned. Real right view changes what the mind does; intellectual right view only changes what the mouth says.

questions.q: Who is the "noble disciple" (ariyasāvaka) this sutta repeatedly addresses?
questions.a: Not an arahant — the formula works for anyone at stream-entry or beyond, and arguably for anyone practicing in that direction. The noble disciple is the one who has grasped the four-truth pattern deeply enough that it now shapes how they see any phenomenon. "Noble" here is functional, not honorific.
